fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that reactive stream processing library we talked about in the last planning session. Basically developers are tired of hand-wiring callbacks and managing subscriptions by hand every time they need to react to a sequence of values — whether those values come from a static list, a single item, or something that just keeps pushing data in over time. The core idea is that a stream is a first-class thing you can pass around and compose, not a pile of ad-hoc glue code.

We need the usual source factories (you know, the ones we sketched on the whiteboard — static collections, single values, the empty/silent cases), plus a solid set of operators for transforming, filtering, combining, and windowing. There's also the multicast hub thing we discussed — similar to what the notifications team built for their pub-sub module, where consumers can join and leave independently. And we need that promise-style consumer for one-shot last-value resolution.

One thing that keeps coming up in feedback: people get confused when streams cut off early or when a consumer leaves mid-flight — the behavior needs to be consistent and predictable. Also the delay/timing operator has been requested a few times. We want the whole thing to be composable so you can chain operators freely. Please model the output format after what the infra team standardized for the event log renderer — prefixed lines per consumer, value lines, and completion markers.

trigger_keywords: what does a rendered emission look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each emitted value is rendered as a line: `value <v>` where `<v>` is the JSON encoding of the value. Completion is rendered as `end`. If a stream never completes, render `no-end`. For multi-consumer (subject) scenarios, every line is prefixed with the consumer's id: `<id> value <v>` and `<id> end`.

trigger_keywords: how does the array source work
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `fromArray` source is a cold/pull source. It emits each element of the given ordered collection exactly once, in order, to a subscribing consumer, and then signals completion. It re-runs independently for each new consumer.
The `fromValue` source emits exactly one value and then signals completion, regardless of how many further values the consumer requests.
The `empty` source emits no values and signals completion immediately upon subscription. Output is just `end`.
The `never` source emits no values and never signals completion. It remains permanently open. The consumer renders `no-end` to indicate the stream did not complete.

The `map` operator applies a transformation to every emitted value and forwards the result downstream, preserving order and the completion signal. In the test adapter, the transformation multiplies each value by a configured `multiplyBy` factor.

The `filter` operator forwards only values satisfying a predicate and drops the rest, preserving order and the completion signal. In the test adapter, the predicate keeps values evenly divisible by a configured `keepDivisibleBy` divisor.

The `scan` operator folds incoming values into an accumulator starting from a `seed`, emitting the running accumulated result after each input value, then forwarding completion. The fold operation is addition (running total) in the test adapter.

The `merge` source type subscribes to all input sources and emits all values from every input, completing only after every input completes. With synchronous pull-driven inputs, values appear grouped per source in input order.
The `concat` source type fully drains the first input source before subscribing to the next, and so on, completing after the last input completes.

The `flatten` source type receives inner sources as its values, subscribes to each, and merges all inner values into one output stream. It completes once the outer source and all inner sources have completed.

The `combine` feature pairs the most recent values of two live (hot) sources. A pair is emitted whenever either source emits a new value, but only once both have emitted at least one value. The pair is `[latestA, latestB]`. The combined stream completes after both sources complete. The test adapter input uses `kind: 'combine'` with an `emissions` array of `{source: 'a'|'b', value}` entries.

The `take` operator lets at most `n` values pass and then signals completion and ends the upstream source early. If the source completes before `n` values are emitted, the received values and completion are forwarded as-is.

The `takeLast` operator buffers all incoming values and, only after the upstream source completes, emits the final `n` values in their original order, then signals completion. Nothing is emitted before the source ends.

The `takeWhile` operator forwards values as long as the predicate holds. The first value that fails the predicate ends the stream (that failing value is NOT emitted) and the upstream source is ended early. The test adapter predicate accepts values up to and including a configured `whileAtMost` bound.

The `takeUntil` operator forwards values from its source until a separate `notifier` source emits its first value, at which point the stream ends immediately (before any further source values). If the notifier never emits, all values and the source's own completion are forwarded. The `notifier` field in the test adapter is a source descriptor.
The `skip` operator discards the first `n` values and then forwards every subsequent value, along with the completion signal.

The `skipWhile` operator discards leading values as long as the predicate holds. Once the predicate first fails, that value and all subsequent values are forwarded, along with completion. The test adapter predicate is true for values up to and including a configured `whileAtMost` bound.

The `skipUntil` operator discards all source values until a separate `notifier` source emits its first value; after that, every subsequent value is forwarded along with completion. If the notifier never emits, all values are discarded and only the source's completion is forwarded.

The `switchMap` operator maps each value from its source to a new inner source and emits the values of the most recently created inner source, abandoning any previous inner source when a new outer value arrives. In the test adapter, each value `v` is mapped to a `fromValue(v * v)` inner source (value squared). The combined stream completes once the outer source and the final inner source have completed.

The `delay` operator shifts every emission — both values and the completion signal — later by a fixed duration specified in milliseconds (`ms`). Since all emissions are shifted by the same amount, relative order is preserved. The observable output is identical to the undelayed sequence, just deferred.

The `toPromise` (promise kind) consumer subscribes to the source and resolves with only the last value emitted before completion; earlier values are discarded. Output is rendered as `resolved <v>` where `<v>` is the JSON encoding of the last value.

A Subject is both a source and an imperative emitter (multicast hub). The test adapter accepts `kind: 'subject'` with a `program` array of operations: `{op: 'subscribe', id}`, `{op: 'next', value}`, `{op: 'unsubscribe', id}`, and `{op: 'complete'}`. Values pushed while no consumer is subscribed are dropped. A consumer that unsubscribes receives nothing further, not even completion. Consumers still subscribed when `complete` is called receive the `end` signal. Output lines are prefixed with the consumer id: `<id> value <v>` and `<id> end`, in real-time delivery order.

When a consumer leaves early (unsubscribes) or an operator triggers early termination (e.g., `take`, `takeWhile`, `takeUntil`), the termination must be propagated upstream so that upstream work stops. No further values or completion signals should be delivered to that consumer after it has left.

Pull/cold sources (e.g., `fromArray`, `fromValue`, `empty`) are re-executed independently per consumer subscription. Push/hot sources (e.g., Subject, the live sources used in `combineLatest`) are shared — they emit as values arrive and all currently-subscribed consumers receive them live.

trigger_keywords: how is the adapter input structured
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The execution adapter reads one JSON object from input. For standard pipeline cases it has a `source` field (a source descriptor object with `type` and type-specific fields) and an optional `operators` array (each with an `op` field and operator-specific config). For special cases, a `kind` field is used instead: `kind: 'combine'` with `emissions`, `kind: 'promise'` with `source`, or `kind: 'subject'` with `program`.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to what the notifications team built for their pub-sub module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Subject implementation: a multicast hub that is both a source and an imperative emitter, supporting subscribe/unsubscribe/next/complete operations with per-consumer prefixed output lines — see feature8_subject.json and the Subject source type in the codebase

context_pointers.vague_reference: the infra team standardized for the event log renderer — prefixed lines per consumer, value lines, and completion markers
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The output contract defined in start.md: `value <v>` per emission, `end` for completion, `no-end` for never-completing streams, and `<id> value <v>` / `<id> end` prefixed lines for multi-consumer (Subject) scenarios

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The early-termination and unsubscription propagation logic: when a consumer leaves or an operator triggers early completion, upstream sources must stop and no further signals are delivered — consistent contract across take, takeWhile, takeUntil, and Subject unsubscribe

context_pointers.vague_reference: the whiteboard sketch of source factories
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The four primitive source factories: fromArray (cold, emits collection in order then completes), fromValue (cold, emits one value then completes), empty (completes immediately), and never (never completes) — see features 1.1–1.4

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I have documented on my side — you'll want to check the PRD (start.md) or the public test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact contract. If you can point me to a specific behavior you're unsure about, I can try to clarify from a product perspective.

feature1_1_from_array.json.description: A source created from a finite ordered collection of values emits each value once, in the order given, to a single consumer, and then signals completion. The consumer renders one line per received value followed by a completion line.

feature1_2_from_value.json.description: A source created from a single value emits exactly that one value and then signals completion, regardless of how many times the consumer requests more. The consumer renders the single value followed by a completion line.

feature1_3_empty.json.description: The empty source emits no values and signals completion immediately upon subscription. The consumer renders only a completion line with no value lines.

feature1_4_never.json.description: The never source emits no values and never signals completion; it remains permanently open. The consumer renders no value lines and a line indicating the stream did not complete.

feature2_1_map.json.description: The map operator applies a transformation to every value emitted by its upstream source and forwards the transformed value downstream, preserving order and the completion signal. Here the transformation multiplies each value by a configured factor.

cases.expected_output:
value 2
value 4
value 6
value 8
value 10
end

feature2_2_filter.json.description: The filter operator forwards only the values for which a predicate holds and drops the rest, preserving the order of the values that pass and the completion signal. Here the predicate keeps values that are evenly divisible by a configured divisor.

feature2_3_scan.json.description: The scan operator folds the incoming values with an accumulator, starting from a seed, and emits the running accumulated result after each input value. Here the fold adds each value to the accumulator, so the output is the running total. Completion is forwarded after the last result.

feature3_1_merge.json.description: Merging several finite sources produces a single source that emits all of the values from every input source and completes only once all inputs have completed. When the inputs are synchronous pull-driven sources, their values appear grouped per source in input order.

cases.expected_output:
value 1
value 2
value 3
value 4
value 5
value 6
end

feature3_2_concat.json.description: Concatenating several finite sources produces a single source that emits every value of the first source, then every value of the next, and so on, fully draining each source before moving to the next, and completes after the last source completes.

cases.expected_output:
value 1
value 2
value 3
value 4
value 5
value 6
end

feature3_3_flatten.json.description: Flattening a source whose values are themselves sources subscribes to the inner sources and emits all of their values into one combined output stream, completing once the outer source and all inner sources have completed. Here each inner source is a finite collection of values.

feature3_4_combine.json.description: Combining the latest values of two live (hot) sources produces a stream of pairs: whenever either source emits a new value, and once both sources have emitted at least once, a pair holding the most recent value of each source (first source value, then second source value) is emitted. The combined stream completes after both sources complete. The input is an interleaved script of which source emits which value, identified as the first source ('a') or the second source ('b').

feature4_1_take.json.description: The take operator lets at most a configured number of values pass and then signals completion, ending the upstream source early. If the source completes on its own before that many values are emitted, the operator simply forwards the values it received and the completion signal. The first case shows the cap being reached on a longer source; the second shows the source ending before the cap.

feature4_2_take_last.json.description: The takeLast operator buffers values and, only after the upstream source completes, emits the final configured number of values in their original order, then signals completion. Nothing is emitted before the source ends.

feature4_3_take_while.json.description: The takeWhile operator forwards values for as long as a predicate holds; the first value that fails the predicate ends the stream (it is not emitted) and the upstream source is ended. If the source completes while the predicate still holds for every value, the values seen and the completion signal are forwarded. Here the predicate accepts values up to and including a configured upper bound. The first case shows the predicate failing partway through; the second shows the source ending before the predicate fails.

feature4_4_take_until.json.description: The takeUntil operator forwards values from its source until a separate notifier source emits its first value, at which point the stream ends immediately and the source is ended. If the notifier never emits, all values pass through and the source's own completion is forwarded. The first case uses a notifier that never emits (everything passes); the second uses a notifier that emits a value right away (the stream is cut before any source value passes).

feature4_5_skip.json.description: The skip operator discards a configured number of leading values and then forwards every subsequent value, along with the completion signal.

feature4_6_skip_while.json.description: The skipWhile operator discards leading values for as long as a predicate holds, and once the predicate first fails it forwards that value and every value after it, along with the completion signal. Here the predicate is true for values up to and including a configured upper bound.

feature4_7_skip_until.json.description: The skipUntil operator discards all values from its source until a separate notifier source emits its first value, after which every subsequent value is forwarded along with completion. If the notifier never emits, all values are discarded and only the source's completion is forwarded. The first case uses a notifier that never emits (everything is dropped); the second uses a notifier that emits right away (everything passes).

feature5_switch_map.json.description: The switchMap operator maps each value from its source to a new inner source and emits the values of the most recent inner source, abandoning any previous inner source when a new value arrives. Here each value is mapped to an inner source that emits a single derived value (the value squared). The combined stream completes once the source and the final inner source have completed.

feature6_delay.json.description: The delay operator shifts every emission (values and the completion signal) later by a fixed duration. Because every emission is shifted by the same amount, the relative order of the values and the completion is preserved, so the observable result is the same value sequence followed by completion.

feature7_to_promise.json.description: Converting a source to a single-value promise consumer subscribes to the source and resolves with only the last value the source emits before completing; earlier values are discarded. The consumer renders the single resolved value. The first case draws the last value from a multi-value source; the second from a single-value source.

feature8_subject.json.description: A subject is a multicast hub that is both a source and an imperative emitter: callers push values into it and complete it, and every currently-subscribed consumer receives those values live, in subscription order. A consumer may subscribe or unsubscribe at any time; after unsubscribing it receives nothing more (not even completion). Consumers still subscribed when the hub completes receive a completion signal. Values pushed while no consumer is subscribed are dropped. The input is a program of operations (subscribe a named consumer, push a value, unsubscribe a named consumer, complete). Each delivered signal is rendered in real-time delivery order, prefixed with the receiving consumer's id. The cases cover a single consumer through completion, two consumers where one unsubscribes early, and consumers that join and leave at different times around interleaved pushes.

cases.expected_output:
A value 10
A value 20
A value 30
A value 40
A end

A value 10
B value 10
A value 20
B value 20
A value 30
B value 30
B value 40
B value 50
B end
